title: Crushed Apples and a Balance Scale

content.problemStatement:
You are preparing for meals ahead of your busy night at Restaurant Deep Dishes. An essential ingredient is apples.

In your inventory, you have MMM kilograms of crushed apples. Head Chef Sandeep needs exactly NNN kilograms of crushed apples, but Chef Pal has lost the weighing machine. Instead, all that is left is a balance pan with two scales. You do not have any measuring weights with you either.

So you can only place some crushed apples on both sides of the balance, and the balance will tell you whether or not they are of equal weight. You don't get any more information from it.

And peculiarly, it gives out an error if both sides are not integers. You don't know if they are equal or not in this case.

In other words, this is what you can do:


You can take a known weight of apples and divide into two equal parts using the balance, if the two halves have integer weights.
You can take crushed apples of a known integer weight and get another portion of crushed apples that weighs the same, using the balance. Of course you cannot do this if are exceeding the available MMM kilograms.
And of course, even without using the balance, you can combine together known weights to get a heavier known weight.
Chef Pal is asking for your help. Under these constraints, is it possible to get exactly NNN kilograms of crushed apples? Note that when you start out, the only weight that you know is that the entire crushed apples weights MMM kilograms.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains one integer TTT, the number of test cases. The test cases follow.
The only line of each testcase contains two integers MMM and NNN, the kilograms of apples you have, and the kilograms of apples that you need to make, respectively.


Output Format

For each test case, if it is possible to use the balance scale to get exactly NNN kilograms from MMM kilograms, output YES.
If it is not possible, output NO.

Note: You can output the answer in any case (upper or lower). For example, the strings yEs, yes, Yes, and YES will be recognized as positive responses.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers M and N – the total kilograms of crushed apples you have and the kilograms you need respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to obtain exactly N kilograms using the described operations, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Testcase 1: M=2 (odd=1). N=1 is multiple of 1 and ≤2 → YES.
Testcase 2: M=1 (odd=1). N=1 → YES.
Testcase 3: M=5 (odd=5). N=1 is not a multiple of 5 → NO.
Testcase 4: N> M → NO.

M=12=3·2^2, odd=3.
9 is 3·3 (multiple of 3) and ≤12 → YES (6+3).
8 is not a multiple of 3 → NO.
6 is 3·2 → YES.
M is odd (odd=7). Only weight 7 can be produced. N=7 → YES, N=14 > M → NO.

hidden.label: large even M, N not multiple of odd part

solution.approach: Let M = odd × 2^a where odd is the odd part of M (remove all factors of 2). Using the balance we can repeatedly halve any even known weight, therefore we can obtain exactly the set of weights {odd·2^k | 0 ≤ k ≤ a}. Because we can duplicate any known weight arbitrarily (as long as the total does not exceed M) and we can sum known weights, the problem reduces to: can N be expressed as a sum of these powers of two multiplied by odd, without exceeding M? This is possible iff N is a multiple of odd and N ≤ M. The condition is both necessary (every obtainable weight is a multiple of odd) and sufficient (write N/odd in binary and use the corresponding powers of two, each of which is available). The algorithm for each test case: 1) Compute odd = M while even, i.e., repeatedly divide M by 2 until it becomes odd. 2) If N ≤ M and N % odd == 0 output YES, else NO. The computation is O(log M) for the division loop, which is ≤ 60 iterations for 64‑bit numbers, effectively O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T · log M) → effectively O(T) because log M ≤ 60.
